data.epi_content:
I don't know how many days have passed.

There is no day or night here.

Only a single girl exists in this place.

Death doesn't exist here.

Even as I writhe in hunger, thinking it would be better to die, trying to waste away, I curse the cruel mercy falling from above as I stick out my tongue to catch and drink the water.

There's hardly anything unpleasant here.

The most unpleasant thing is realizing that Miss Erica is not the cruel person I imagined—not someone who harbors resentment toward the world, but rather an insignificant human who masturbates by smashing the heads of worthless insects like Lydia.

I'm not the extraordinary person I imagined—cruel, cold, capable of blowing someone's head off with a bullet at any moment. I'm nothing but an awkward, worm-like being, no different from Lydia.

I couldn't become a villain or even a despicable person. I'm neither a cold-blooded assassin nor an honest, good person—it's laughable. It's more appropriate to call me an undeveloped fetus-like worm.

In this tiny room, I keep going back in time, repeating ridiculous thoughts: that I'm somehow different from others with a useless sense of being chosen, that wise and clever people accomplish nothing, and that only fools blessed by God can become something.

Come to think of it, I can't even be a mere worm.

Worms eat when they want, spread filth as they please, and enjoy clinging to others, but I can't even do that—I'm just a starving something trapped in a room.

With my mouth covered, I drink water through my nostrils and find joy in it—how horrifying...

It feels like three weeks have passed.

Perhaps it's been only three days.

It feels like a month has passed.

Perhaps it's been only a day.

The ambiguity of time, hunger, fatigue, and the pain of broken bones were enough to make my mind confused and vulnerable.

At such times, in my consciousness, I tear the Crown Prince to death, curse Evan for not saving me, burn Vivian alive, and dismember Lydia.

The more I imagine, the more the pain in my body transforms the self-harm riddled with humiliation into sweetness.

Then I bring pleasure to myself in this white, soft place that provides just enough moisture and doesn't reject me!

Some might question if I'm just a mentally ill person spreading my imaginary wings and grinning stupidly, but I insist again—this is pleasure itself!

Since there's no one else here to oppose my opinion, my word must be right.

In truth, I know better than anyone that these are nonsensical ravings.

But in the end, I'm the only one in this space who can express and accept opinions.

Just me. Just me. Just me.

Damn it, I'm alone.

When I speak, I am the law, and when I accept something, it becomes universal acceptance.

Therefore, since I am practically the entirety of this space, my pride might be sky-high.

Like a disabled person with missing limbs, or a mentally ill person who trembles at loud sounds thinking bombs are exploding!

They have nothing, yet they're full of pride as they recall what they've been through while finding it horrifying.

Let me out.

I said let me out.

It's painful.

Please, anyone, get me out of here.

I crawl along the floor, limping, and bang my head against where the door should be several times.

The door doesn't open.

Isn't it common sense to open the door when someone knocks?

Perhaps another day has passed.
data.epi_content2:
The room is dirty.

The room is dirty.

No, I'm not dirty, so what comes from my body isn't dirty either.

Therefore, the room isn't dirty.

But why do I want to escape from here?

Humans are mortal beings.

That may be true.

But even if we perish, let's resist while perishing.

And even if what awaits us is emptiness, let's not make everything return to rightness.

Does this mean I must accept that rebellion proves it's the movement of life, that one cannot deny rebellion without giving up on living?

In this terrible situation—twisting my whole body, receiving water drops from above through my nostrils, unable to cough because my mouth is blocked—must I accept that I need to endure and keep rebelling?

Those damn romanticists who eat snails are good at saying pretty things.

At least they've never experienced being locked in a room, not knowing how much time has passed, drinking water through their nose for days.

When that thought occurred to me, I laughed because they seemed so pathetic.

But thanks to whatever is stuck in my mouth, it's hard even to lift the corners of my lips.

I bang my head frantically against the floor.

Since it's soft, it just feels cozy.

Although I've done this many times before, I start to feel disappointed and desperate again.

I twist my entire body.

Having been beaten all over, with no part of my body unharmed, I can't move properly.

I try to scream.

My mouth is blocked.

Since my mouth is open, my jaw has no strength, and my tongue is pressed down, so I can't do anything.

I never thought simply confining someone could torment them like this.

I almost think it might be better to be buried in a shell crater, trembling in fear of death while explosions rage around me.

Despite everything, the image of Evan, who once saved me while muttering through tears, begins to clear my dizzy mind.

But I start to feel miserable again, realizing Evan is my only thread of hope.

I wish I could just die.

Though the Crown Prince wouldn't allow that.

Should I have blown my own head off instead of shooting the Crown Prince in that moment of rage?

But even after experiencing all this, I would clearly make the same choice if such a situation arose again.

I'm not a person who acts on reason or rationality—I'm just a worthless human who moves according to emotions depending on the situation.

Yes, I'm just having bad luck now.

Bad luck. Terribly bad luck.

What did I do wrong, what evil deed did I commit to deserve this treatment?

Then, the door opened.

And the Crown Prince, whom I resented so much, begins to look like a savior—a realization so cruel that I start to cry.

The experiences of dying repeatedly and enduring a painful life were easily trampled by simply being confined and given only water.

My will was this pathetic and meaningless.
data.epi_content3:
"I'm glad that fierce look in your eyes has somewhat faded. It's always enjoyable to see pupils clouding with fear of the door closing again when you glare."

Saying this, he approaches me but wrinkles his nose and covers it when he smells the odor from the room.

"First, wash her. Then bring her to that room."

At his words, the beautiful woman with doll-like eyes standing behind the Crown Prince nods and approaches me.

She picks me up as if I were a child, seemingly unbothered by my filth, and begins carrying me somewhere.

Since falling into this place, I've always been dragged around by people as if my will means nothing.

Whether it's Vivian, Evan, the Crown Prince, Lydia, or strangers.

As if I can't do anything.

The woman glances around and begins to speak to me quietly.

She seems to be checking if we're far enough that the Crown Prince can't hear.

"I don't know if you'll become a flower like me or furniture like the child who came in before, but you've certainly been destined for an unfortunate life."

My throat was too parched to answer.

I was simply moved by the warmth of the person holding me and the endless corridor stretching out after leaving that room.

She gently places me in a bathtub and begins washing me with warm water.

Despite all the disgusting smells rising up, she shows no reaction and continues washing my body with delicate hands.

The distinctive stench of dried and rotting blood, the smell of filth, my dried, twisted mouth, and dirty hair.

The merciful water begins to wash it all away.

"Child, what's your name?"

"...Erica."

Even after drinking water, perhaps because I hadn't eaten anything, my words didn't come out properly.

"What a pretty name."

Though the meaning of the name has completely disappeared.

"Erica, Erica."

She murmurs my name for a long time while washing me, then brings a towel to dry my body.

Next, she moves me to a dressing area and brings several modest outfits for me to choose from.

She tells me to select one and put it on.

In the dressing area, there were several full-length mirrors.

Looking at myself, where my right eye should have been, there was only something rotten, and my body was completely emaciated with ribs and pelvis showing.

I don't have the strength to dress myself.

So I put on a modest white outfit and sat on the floor.

I'm ugly.

Not beautiful.

My facial bones are gauntly exposed.

Wondering if I might look a little prettier if I smiled, if it might lessen the ugliness, I tried smiling.

I was still ugly.

So I turned my head away.
